title: resolveDecoderProgressiveDetail disables progressive for single-pass sessions that have an explicit progressiveDetail set
description: The condition on line 434 reads `!(options.progressionTarget !== 'final' || options.emitEveryPass)`, which is De Morgan equivalent to `options.progressionTarget === 'final' && !options.emitEveryPass`. However, this check fires even when `options.progressiveDetail` is defined (the surrounding `if` only checks `progressiveDetail === undefined`). An explicit `progressiveDetail` such as `lastPasses` must subscribe to libjxl progressive events even when emitEveryPass=false ‚Äî the comment on line 431 actually says this ‚Äî but the condition can also short-circuit to 0 for progressionTarget='pass' with emitEveryPass=false when progressiveDetail is absent, which silently requests a non-progressive decode while callers expect at least one pass event.

title: Progressive decoder pushes batchBytes from before-drain queue but drains chunks inside the batch loop, causing woff to drift from actual bytes written
description: At line 1515 `batchBytes` is captured as `this.queuedBytes` before the copy loop. Inside the loop, `this.queuedBytes` is decremented per chunk (line 1533). If the chunk sizes sum to less than `batchBytes` (because another consumer or re-entrant call modified `queuedBytes` ‚Äî theoretically impossible in single-thread JS, but practically because the final `null` sentinel may not be present), the WASM push call at line 1539 passes `batchBytes` (original total) rather than the actual `woff` bytes written into the heap buffer. This means extra uninitialized bytes are fed to the decoder. In normal operation this is consistent; it becomes wrong if `woff !== batchBytes` which cannot happen in the current single-threaded path, but any future change to the queue draining could silently corrupt decode. The safe fix is `decPush(dec, chunkBufPtr, woff)` instead of `decPush(dec, chunkBufPtr, batchBytes)`.
code_excerpt:
result = decPush(dec, chunkBufPtr, batchBytes);
          if (result < 0) throw new Error(`JXL decode error: ${decError(dec)}`);

title: Warm-up in benchmarkModule fires only once across all files in all scenarios, not once per file
description: The `warmed` flag is set the first time ANY file in any scenario is processed, meaning only the very first file of the first scenario gets a warm-up run. All subsequent files ‚Äî including the first file of later scenarios ‚Äî run without warm-up. The intent (suggested by the variable name and placement) is to warm up the JIT once per module load, which is technically achieved, but if the intent is per-scenario or per-file warm-up the flag is misplaced. Additionally `warmed` is initialised outside the per-scenario loop so when the benchmark is reused across multiple calls to `benchmarkModule`, the `warmed` flag will never be reset.

title: JXTC tile-seam blend formula is asymmetric: left tile heavily weighted (179/256) and right tile lightly weighted (77/256), then both pixels set to the same blended value ‚Äî tiles closer to the seam on their respective sides are treated identically
description: The blend formula `(l*179 + r*77 + 128) >> 8` assigns weight 179/256 ‚âà 0.70 to the left pixel and 77/256 ‚âà 0.30 to the right pixel. Both the left and right pixel are then written back to the same blended value. A symmetric seam blend would use equal weights (128/128) or at minimum ensure the left column gets a left-biased value and the right column gets a right-biased value (e.g. left = lerp(l,r, 0.25), right = lerp(l,r, 0.75)). Using the same asymmetric blend for both means the seam is displaced rather than smoothed: the right column pixel effectively uses the wrong interpolation direction.

title: Horizontal seam blend in JXTC uses variables named 't' and 'b' (top/bottom) that shadow the outer blend variable 'blended', hiding a copy-paste inconsistency
description: The horizontal seam loop uses `t` and `b` for top/bottom pixel values, but the blend result is stored in `blended`. Both pixels are then set to `blended`. This is structurally identical to the vertical seam bug (finding logic-4), but additionally the naming `b` for a pixel value in the inner loop `for (int c = 0; c < 4; ++c)` shadows the outer-scope blend variable at the same level, making the code misleading. Critically, the same asymmetric 179/77 weighting applies: `(t * 179 + b * 77 + 128) >> 8`. The top pixel gets 70% weight and the bottom gets 30%, yet both rows are set to the identical value.

title: encodeRgba8 passes the same metaPtr three times for all three metadata slots (ICC, EXIF, XMP)
description: When metadata bytes are present and the `_jxl_wasm_encode_rgba8_with_metadata` bridge function exists, the function allocates a single `metaPtr` and copies `metadataBytes` into it, then passes this same pointer and size for all three metadata fields: icc_ptr, exif_ptr, and xmp_ptr. This is almost certainly a copy-paste error ‚Äî the bridge expects three separate buffers for ICC profile, EXIF, and XMP data. The PGO trainer (pgo-train.mjs) correctly uses separate `iccPtr`, `exifPtr`, `xmpPtr` allocations. This means PGO benchmark profiles use incorrect metadata when testing the `encode-container` scenario.

title: pgo-train.mjs calls pathToFileUrl() before it is defined ‚Äî function declaration hoisting does not apply to functions defined later in the module
description: Line 14 calls `pathToFileUrl(moduleJs)` at the module's top-level execution scope (outside any async function), before the function definition at line 245. In Node.js ES modules, function declarations ARE hoisted, but `pathToFileUrl` is defined as a `function` declaration (not an arrow function or `const`), so it should be hoisted. However, the top-level call at line 14 is an `await import(...)` inside a module-level statement ‚Äî if this file is executed as a script, `pathToFileUrl` is accessible due to hoisting. The real risk is that `pathToFileUrl` uses a hand-rolled URL encoding that may fail on Windows paths with drive letters (e.g., `C:/...` ‚Üí `file:///C:/...` is correct, but `C:\foo` after `replaceAll('\\', '/')` produces `C:/foo` and then `file:///C:/foo` which is correct). Double-check: paths starting with a drive letter like `C:` must NOT get `file:///` prepended without a leading `/`. Currently line 247 does: `normalized.startsWith('/') ? ... : 'file:///' + normalized` ‚Äî for `C:/foo`, this yields `file:///C:/foo` which is correct. The logic appears sound but this is a fragile custom implementation vs `pathToFileURL` from Node's `url` module.

title: passOrdinal emitted on progress events is flushCount-1 (zero-based), but on final it is flushCount (also zero-based counting the final as a pass), creating an inconsistent ordinal across event types
description: In eventsProgressive, progress events set `passOrdinal: flushCount - 1` (line 1621) meaning the first flush (DC) has passOrdinal=0. But the synthetic progress event emitted when no real flush occurred (line 1690) uses `passOrdinal: flushCount` (which is 0 at that point, matching). The final event uses `passOrdinal: flushCount` (line 1707). This means for a 2-pass image: pass0 progress event has passOrdinal=0, pass1 progress event has passOrdinal=1, and final event has passOrdinal=2. The progression is consistent. However the comment in the code says 'first flush is the DC pass' and flushCount starts at 0 then is incremented to 1 before this point ‚Äî so passOrdinal for the DC pass is 0, which is correct. The issue is that consumers comparing passOrdinal across progress and final events may be confused: the final event's passOrdinal equals flushCount (number of flushes), not the last pass index. This is a semantic inconsistency, not a crash.

title: jxtcNonTileRanges reads tilesX at offset 20 and tilesY at offset 24, but JXTC header layout puts tilesX at offset 20 (h32[5]) and tilesY at offset 24 (h32[6]) ‚Äî correct ‚Äî but tileCount is not cross-checked against tiles_x*tiles_y overflow
description: The JXTC header reads tilesX from offset 20 (h32[5]) and tilesY from offset 24 (h32[6]), which matches the C++ layout (h32[5]=tiles_x, h32[6]=tiles_y). However, `tileCount = tilesX * tilesY` can silently overflow for very large tile counts (both are uint32; JS number handles up to 2^53 safely so no overflow, but a zero check after multiplication via `Number.isSafeInteger` is the guard). The minimum size check at line 2692 is `view.byteLength < 40` but the JXTC header is 32 bytes + the minimum index is 8 bytes per tile. The check `< 40` allows a 1-tile container (32 + 8 = 40), so the minimum is correct. However, when reading tile offsets at line 2705, `32 + i * 8` for the first tile uses offset 32 which is JXTC_HEADER_BYTES ‚Äî consistent. No logic bug found in offset arithmetic specifically, lowering to info level. This is informational.

title: LibjxlEncoder.chunks() byte total check will throw on zero-pixel edge case with empty encoder (0 queuedBytes vs 0 pixelByteTotal)
description: Line 2084 checks `this.queuedPixelBytes !== this.pixelByteTotal` and throws if they differ. For a completely empty image (width=0 or height=0) this would be 0 === 0 and pass. But `expectedPixelBytes` at line 2631 throws for width<=0 or height<=0, so the encoder constructor already rejects these. The real edge case is: if `finish()` is called without any `pushPixels()` and `pixelByteTotal > 0`, this check will throw 'expected N bytes, got 0'. This is correct behavior. No bug here ‚Äî this is informational.

title: distanceFromQuality returns distance=1 for quality=null instead of the JXL lossless distance (0.0) or documented default
description: When `quality` is null, `distanceFromQuality` returns 1 (line 2647), meaning distance=1. Distance=0 means lossless; distance=1 is approximately quality 95 in JPEG terms. Returning 1 for null quality is a reasonable default, but it is undocumented and may surprise callers who pass `quality: null` expecting lossless output. The EncoderOptions interface (line 145) shows both `distance: number | null` and `quality: number | null`, and in chunks() the encoder calls `const distance = this.options.distance ?? distanceFromQuality(this.options.quality)`. If both are null, the encoder silently uses distance=1 (lossy). This is a default value choice that may produce unexpected lossy output when neither distance nor quality is set.

title: The else-if branch 'inputClosed && result === 0' after result===2 done check is unreachable because drainPending would already be true
description: After the main decode loop body, if `result === 0` (NEED_MORE) and `inputClosed` is true, line 1635 throws an error. However, when input is closed (line 1509), `result = decPush(dec, 0, 0)` is called and if that returns 0, on the next iteration `drainPending` is false (reset at 1631) and `inputClosed` is true. This branch CAN be reached if decPush returns 0 after close. But `drainPending` is only set true on result===1, and reset to false on result===0. So after close, if the decoder keeps returning 0 indefinitely, the loop will keep sending empty pushes until it errors or the decoder returns 2. The error path at line 1635 would only trigger if result is neither 1 nor 2 after input was closed AND drainPending is false, which seems like the right safety net. This is not a dead branch but rather a specific error condition that is correctly handled.

title: No test for the 'rgb8' format path in expectedPixelBytes ‚Äî channels=3 computation is only for rgb8 but PixelFormat type admits it in encode only
description: The `expectedPixelBytes` function correctly uses `channels = format === 'rgb8' ? 3 : 4` but there are no property-based or unit tests verifying that rgb8 pixel byte totals are calculated correctly end-to-end through pushPixels/chunks. A 1√ó1 rgb8 frame should be 3 bytes but an rgba8 frame is 4 bytes ‚Äî a mismatch would cause the over-bytes guard in pushPixels to reject valid rgb8 data. The opportunity is to add an invariant test that pushes exactly `w*h*3` bytes for rgb8 format and verifies chunks() completes without error.
code_excerpt:
const channels = format === "rgb8" ? 3 : 4;
  const bytes = width * height * channels * bpc;

title: JXTC tile container index cursor uses uint32_t which can overflow for containers >4 GB total
description: In `EncodeRgba8TileContainer`, `cursor` is declared as `uint32_t` (line 1682). Each tile's bytes are added to cursor (`cursor += static_cast<uint32_t>(tile_lengths[i])`). For a large image with many large tiles, `cursor` can overflow `uint32_t` (max ~4 GB). The total_size is computed as `size_t` (64-bit on WASM32 ‚Äî but actually 32-bit per the static_assert). The WASM32 build constrains practical image sizes, but the offset written to the JXTC index is `cursor` which could silently wrap. `total_size` itself is bounded by `size_t` (32-bit on WASM32), so in practice a single-module allocation >4 GB is impossible on WASM32. This is a theoretical issue on WASM64 but the static_assert blocks that. Downgrading confidence accordingly.

title: Progressive decoder batch write uses pre-drain queuedBytes as size argument to decPush but post-drain woff as actual bytes written, creating a latent inconsistency
description: The local `batchBytes` is captured as `this.queuedBytes` at line 1515 before draining the queue. After the inner while-loop, `woff` contains the actual bytes written. In the single-threaded JS execution model these always match because all chunks in the queue are drained completely. However, if the queue contains a null sentinel mid-batch (which would be caught by `!== null` on line 1529), fewer bytes would be written than `batchBytes` indicates. The null sentinel is only at the logical end of the stream (pushed by close()), but it IS possible to have: [chunk, null] in the queue simultaneously if close() was called just before a new chunk woke the loop. In that case, the inner loop stops at null (line 1529 checks `!== null`), but `batchBytes` still equals the full `queuedBytes` including the chunk before null, which is what gets written. So woff == batchBytes for the actual chunks, not counting the null. This specific path is safe. This is informational complexity, not a bug.

title: pickDownsample returns the first factor where downsampled image >= target, but iterates [8,4,2] meaning it always picks the largest possible factor even when a smaller one would fit
description: pickDownsample iterates factors [8, 4, 2] in descending order and returns the first one where `Math.ceil(sourceLongEdge / factor) >= targetLongEdge`. This means it picks the highest downsampling factor (8) whenever the 8x downsampled image still covers the target. But 'covers the target' means the downsampled image is at least as big as the target ‚Äî so an 8x factor is chosen whenever the image at 8x is still larger than the target. This is the correct behavior: use the largest downsampling that still provides enough pixels. However, the comparison `>=` means: if the 8x downsampled image exactly equals the target, factor 8 is returned, which is correct. The logic is sound for its purpose of picking the coarsest downsample that still meets the minimum resolution.
